scope_periods.description: Unless otherwise noted, our work will concentrate on financial information for the last three fiscal years (“FY19, “FY20”and “FY21”) and the most recent trailing twelve month period ending September 2022.

sections.heading: Financial Overview
bullets: Summarize and comment on the key Target’s accounting processes:
Finance function, and financial reporting framework (including process, timing, structure of financial reporting, and interaction with the information/accounting systems);
Understanding of key differences between US GAAP and IFRS, including pro forma view highlighting the impact on operating income, net income, EPS, etc.
Key indicators of financial performance;
Understand Target’s accounting of intercompany transactions and consolidation process; and
Significant accounting policies and estimates and contemplated changes.

sections.heading: Quality of Earnings
bullets: Summarize potential adjustments identified regarding the profit and loss performance of Target in the form of a quality of earnings analysis, summarizing the risks that may impact earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (“EBITDA”) including:
Leverage Target’s Sell-side quality of earnings analysis as applicable
Impact of COVID on sales trends and expenses, and any corresponding impact relating to inventory provision or write-offs;
Impact of recent input cost increases on EBITDA;
Impact of new locations or closures;
Non-recurring professional fees;
The impact of provisions, estimates, or adjusting entries on the reported results;
The impact of related party transactions (e.g. related party family adjustments to EBITDA, related party leases, etc.); and
Any other large, unusual or non-recurring events or transactions that may have distorted results.

sections.heading: Supporting analysis for quality of earnings
bullets: Obtain and read an analysis of sales and expenses, and enquire about, to the extent available:
Sales and COGS adjustments (including sales returns, markdowns, stock shrinkage, obsolescence, discounts, rebates, other income);
For Retail, understand sales and growth by tonnage;
For Wholesale, understand sales and growth by customer account
Store costs analyzed as a percentage of sales, square footage, or other applicable metric
Evaluate and understand the key trends and dynamics that impact sustainability of revenue and margins;
Evaluate product mix by revenue, costs, and margin to extract key trends that impact revenue forecasts and store performance for both the retail business and the wholesale business
Analyze cross-sell, upsell, retention; cohorts of product category and typical purchasing behaviors (e.g., bundling, etc.)
Understand impact of any private label brands on margin; any differences by location
Evaluate by location to understand gross margin contributions

Analyze business performance, trends, revenue, costs, and margin by location; including typical product category offered at locations that impact the differences in performance – informs locations to rationalize / expand
Evaluate local competitive presence (e.g., number of competitors within 5 or 10km radius circles) that might impact store performance
Integrate with insights from conducting product category analysis
Conduct a ramp-up analysis for recent locations to evaluate any relevant EBITDA adjustments that were identified
Analyze the health of the customer base, including purchasing patterns (e.g., every few days, or week, etc.) and typical purchased / bucketed categories
Leverage target’s customer data (e.g., anonymized) to analyze retention, growth, margin, average order side (AOS), impact of loyalty program, etc.
Analyze metrics such as order frequency, new customers as a percentage of active customers, etc. to understand the health of the customer base
Understand Target’s central production unit and the current sales it supports
Develop analysis in both PowerPoint slides / Excel and PowerBI – create an interactive dashboard

sections.heading: SG&A expenses
bullets: Obtain and read an analysis of the Target’s selling, general and administration expenses and inquire about:
Salaries and wages (including headcount by area and function, if possible);
General and administrative expenses;
Information systems expenses;
Corporate costs;
Extraordinary expenses;
Rent and leases; and
Miscellaneous and other expenses.

sections.heading: Working capital
bullets: Obtain details of the monthly net working capital and comment on the following for Retail, Wholesale, and Corporate Overhead to the extent they can be bifurcated:
The composition of individual working capital accounts (e.g. trade receivables, accounts payable and accruals, inventory and inventory provisions, etc.);
Monthly working capital trends and impact on reported metrics (e.g. days sales outstanding, inventory turns, days payables outstanding, net working capital as a percentage of revenues, etc.);

Large, unusual, or non-operating items and current or upcoming initiatives that may affect normal working capital trends, (such as accruals for bonuses, capital projects, understanding non-trade balances, etc.);
Working capital seasonality and implications on cash flow and working capital peg; and
Understanding the accounting impact of allowance for uncollectible accounts and write-offs, inventory reserve, reserves for sales returns and allowances, and other accruals requiring judgment.

sections.heading: Net debt and other liabilities
bullets: For each of Retail, Wholesale, and Corporate Overhead (to the extent they can be bifurcated):
Analyze reported current and non-current debt and other debt-like items on the balance sheet;
Consider whether elements of working capital have the nature of and may be reclassified as net debt, and if so, summarize the potential impact of these adjustments on working capital and net debt;
Understand whether there are any off-balance sheet liabilities (e.g. incentive plans, legal claims, guarantees, purchase commitments, etc.); and
Other purchase commitments (operating leases) and contingencies (legal matters).

sections.heading: Capital expenditures
bullets: Obtain details of historical capital expenditures, classified as growth and maintenance, by asset category, and comment on:
Understand Target’s real estate profile including owned vs leased;
Growth vs. maintenance capital expenditures, in particular for new store builds, renovations, e-Commerce, IT/Cybersecurity, DCs, and production units
Committed capital expenditures, including recent monthly spending versus budget; and
Historical and planned capital expenditures.

sections.heading: Audit work papers
bullets: Access Target’s FY21 audit work papers and identified audit matters including unrecorded audit differences, control matters, and other salient findings.

sections.heading: Assistance with transaction documentation
bullets: Read relevant asset purchase agreement definitions (e.g. net working capital and debt-like item definitions and adjustment procedures).

industry_specific_terms: store costs as a percentage of sales
reserves for sales returns and allowances
notes: Single source file: Project Tiger - Draft SOW - 2022-09-26.docx. All content extracted from this document.
The SOW is structured around a supermarket/grocery business with both Retail (store operations and e-commerce) and Wholesale business lines, plus Corporate Overhead.
Scope period covers three fiscal years (FY19, FY20, FY21) and trailing twelve months ending September 2022.
No phases (pre-bid/post-bid) are defined in this SOW.

The 'Supporting analysis for quality of earnings' section is notably large and industry-specific, covering store-level analysis, tonnage, product category mix, customer cohort analysis, private label brand impact, central production unit, loyalty program metrics, and PowerBI dashboard creation.
The document references US GAAP to IFRS conversion, suggesting a cross-border or international transaction context.
The SG&A expenses subsection appears after the supporting analysis section and lists standard expense categories (salaries, G&A, information systems, corporate costs, rent/leases).
Working capital analysis is explicitly segmented by Retail, Wholesale, and Corporate Overhead business lines.
Capital expenditure section highlights supermarket-specific items: new store builds, renovations, e-Commerce, IT/Cybersecurity, DCs (distribution centers), and production units.
The SOW includes an 'Assistance with transaction documentation' section focused on asset purchase agreement definitions — indicating this is a buy-side engagement.
